hint: Use 'ich heiße' for 'my name is'.
grammarNote: In German you can say 'ich heiße X' (literally 'I am called X') or 'mein Name ist X'. Both are common.

answer: Guten Morgen! Wie geht es dir?
alternatives: Guten Morgen! Wie geht es Ihnen?
hint: Use 'dir' for informal, 'Ihnen' for formal.
grammarNote: 'Wie geht es dir?' is informal (du-form). 'Wie geht es Ihnen?' is formal (Sie-form).

hint: 'Tschüss' is informal, 'Auf Wiedersehen' is more formal.
grammarNote: 'bis morgen' = 'until tomorrow'. 'bis' takes the accusative for time expressions.

prompt: Guten Abend, schön dich zu sehen.
answer: Good evening, nice to see you.
alternatives: Good evening, nice to see you again.
hint: 'schön' here means 'nice' or 'lovely'.
grammarNote: 'dich zu sehen' is an infinitive clause: 'to see you'. 'dich' is the accusative of 'du'.

hint: 'Vielen Dank' is slightly more formal than 'Danke schön'.
grammarNote: 'Vielen Dank' literally means 'many thanks' — 'Dank' is masculine and 'vielen' is its accusative form.

hint: Literally: 'I come from Germany'.
grammarNote: 'aus' (= from) always takes the dative. Country names are usually used without an article.

hint: 'wohnen' is more common for residence; 'leben' is broader ('to live').
grammarNote: 'in' takes the dative when answering 'wo?' (where) — no movement is implied.

hint: German numbers: units first, then tens, joined by 'und'.
grammarNote: 25 = fünf-und-zwanzig (five-and-twenty). This 'units before tens' pattern holds for all two-digit numbers above 20.

hint: 'ein bisschen' = a little (bit).
grammarNote: Languages are capitalized in German (Deutsch, Englisch). 'sprechen' is a strong verb: ich spreche, du sprichst, er spricht.

answer: What do you do for work?
hint: 'beruflich' = work-related.
grammarNote: 'beruflich' is an adverb derived from 'der Beruf' (profession). A natural English equivalent is 'for work'.

hint: Careful — 'halb acht' means 'half BEFORE eight' (7:30), not 'half past eight'.
grammarNote: Germans count toward the next hour: 'halb acht' = halfway to eight = 7:30. This trips up English speakers constantly.

hint: Literally: 'How late is it?'
grammarNote: 'Wie spät ist es?' is the most common way to ask the time. 'Wie viel Uhr ist es?' is also fine but slightly more formal.

hint: Weekday names are capitalized and masculine (der Montag).
grammarNote: All days of the week are masculine in German: der Montag, der Dienstag, etc. No article needed when used as a time expression.

prompt: Ich habe um neun Uhr einen Termin.
answer: I have an appointment at nine o'clock.
alternatives: I've got an appointment at nine.
hint: 'um ... Uhr' = at ... o'clock.
grammarNote: Time expressions with 'um' take the accusative. 'der Termin' = appointment (masculine, hence 'einen' in accusative).

answer: Der Zug fährt um acht ab.
alternatives: Der Zug fährt um acht Uhr ab.
hint: 'abfahren' is a separable verb — the 'ab' goes to the end.
grammarNote: Separable verbs split in main clauses: 'ich fahre ab', 'der Zug fährt ab'. The prefix moves to sentence end.

prompt: I have one brother and two sisters.
answer: Ich habe einen Bruder und zwei Schwestern.
hint: 'der Bruder' is masculine — accusative 'einen Bruder'.
grammarNote: 'haben' takes accusative. der Bruder → einen Bruder (masc. acc.). die Schwester → die Schwestern (plural, no article change here).

hint: Professions usually drop the article in German.
grammarNote: After 'sein' + profession, German typically omits the article. 'Lehrerin' is the feminine form of 'Lehrer'.

prompt: Mein Vater arbeitet bei einer Bank.
answer: My father works at a bank.
alternatives: My dad works at a bank.
hint: 'bei' + dative.
grammarNote: 'bei' (at/with) takes the dative. 'die Bank' is feminine, so dative singular = 'einer Bank'.

hint: Use 'du' for informal, 'Sie' for formal.
grammarNote: Yes/no questions in German invert verb and subject. 'Kinder' is the plural of 'das Kind'.

hint: 'Oma' is informal for grandmother.
grammarNote: 'die Großmutter' (formal) = 'die Oma' (informal). Munich = München in German.

prompt: I would like a coffee, please.
answer: Ich hätte gern einen Kaffee, bitte.
hint: 'Ich hätte gern' = I would like (polite).
grammarNote: 'Ich hätte gern' is the Konjunktiv II of 'haben' — the standard polite way to order. 'der Kaffee' → 'einen Kaffee' (acc.).

hint: 'das Brot' — bread is neuter.
grammarNote: 'das Brot' is neuter. Note that many basic foods have surprising genders: das Bier, der Wein, die Milch, der Käse.

answer: What would you like to drink?
alternatives: What do you want to drink?
hint: Formal 'Sie' form.
grammarNote: 'möchten' is the polite form for 'want'. Note Sie-form: 'Sie möchten' (same as infinitive — convenient!).

hint: Negate nouns with 'kein', not 'nicht'.
grammarNote: Use 'kein' (= no/not any) to negate nouns without an article or with 'ein'. 'das Fleisch' is neuter, so 'kein Fleisch'.

hint: 'die Rechnung' = bill/check.
grammarNote: In a restaurant, 'Die Rechnung, bitte' or simply 'Zahlen, bitte' both work to ask for the bill.

prompt: I get up at seven o'clock.
answer: Ich stehe um sieben Uhr auf.
hint: 'aufstehen' is separable.
grammarNote: 'aufstehen' splits: ich stehe ... auf. The prefix 'auf' goes to the end of the clause.

prompt: In the evening I read a book.
answer: Am Abend lese ich ein Buch.
hint: Time expression first → verb stays in second position.
grammarNote: Verb-second rule: when a time expression starts the sentence, the verb still comes second, and the subject follows it.

answer: I go for a walk every day.
alternatives: I take a walk every day.
hint: 'spazieren gehen' = to go for a walk.
grammarNote: 'spazieren gehen' is a fixed combination. 'jeden Tag' is accusative because time expressions of duration take accusative.

prompt: After work I go to the gym.
answer: Nach der Arbeit gehe ich ins Fitnessstudio.
alternatives: Nach der Arbeit gehe ich zum Sport.
hint: 'nach' + dative; 'in' + accusative for movement.
grammarNote: 'nach' always takes the dative ('der Arbeit'). 'ins' = in + das (accusative — direction into the gym).

prompt: I go to bed at eleven.
answer: Ich gehe um elf ins Bett.
alternatives: Um elf gehe ich ins Bett.
Ich gehe um elf Uhr ins Bett.
hint: 'ins Bett gehen' = to go to bed.
grammarNote: 'ins Bett' uses accusative (movement into bed). 'das Bett' → 'ins' (in + das contraction).

prompt: How much does the jacket cost?
hint: Use 'kostet' for 'costs'. 'Die Jacke' is feminine.
grammarNote: Use 'wie viel' for uncountable amounts (money) and 'wie viele' for countables. 'Die Jacke' = the jacket.

prompt: I am looking for a black T-shirt.
hint: 'Suchen' = to look for. 'Das T-Shirt' is neuter, so the adjective ending is '-es' after 'ein'.
grammarNote: Adjective endings after 'ein' for a neuter noun in accusative end in '-es': 'ein schwarzes T-Shirt'. 'Das T-Shirt' = the T-shirt.

prompt: Do you have this shirt in size M?
answer: Haben Sie dieses Hemd in Größe M?
alternatives: Haben Sie das Hemd in Größe M?
Gibt es dieses Hemd in Größe M?
hint: 'Sie' is the formal 'you'. 'Das Hemd' is neuter.
grammarNote: Use formal 'Sie' in shops. 'Das Hemd' = the shirt (button-up); 'das T-Shirt' = the T-shirt. 'Die Größe' = the size.

hint: 'Zu' before an adjective means 'too' (excessively).
grammarNote: 'Die Schuhe' is plural — singular is 'der Schuh' (masculine). 'Zu teuer' = too expensive; 'sehr teuer' = very expensive.

hint: Note: 'die Hose' is singular in German (feminine), unlike English 'trousers'.
grammarNote: 'Die Hose' is grammatically singular in German, so the verb is 'kostet', not 'kosten'. 'Der Euro' stays singular after numbers when talking about prices.

hint: 'Wo' = where. 'Der Bahnhof' is masculine.
grammarNote: 'Der Bahnhof' = the train station. Other common direction nouns: 'die Haltestelle' (bus stop), 'der Flughafen' (airport).

answer: Biegen Sie an der Bäckerei links ab.
hint: 'Abbiegen' is a separable verb: the 'ab' goes to the end. 'Die Bäckerei' is feminine.
grammarNote: Separable verbs split in the imperative: 'biegen... ab'. 'An + Dativ' for location: 'an der Bäckerei'. 'Die Bäckerei' = the bakery.

prompt: The supermarket is on the right.
alternatives: Der Supermarkt ist auf der rechten Seite.
hint: 'Rechts' = on the right. 'Der Supermarkt' is masculine.
grammarNote: Direction adverbs: 'links' (left), 'rechts' (right), 'geradeaus' (straight ahead). 'Der Supermarkt' = the supermarket.

prompt: The pharmacy is near my house.
answer: Die Apotheke ist in der Nähe von meinem Haus.
alternatives: Die Apotheke ist in der Nähe meines Hauses.
Die Apotheke ist nahe bei meinem Haus.
hint: 'In der Nähe von + Dativ' = near. 'Die Apotheke' (feminine) = pharmacy; 'das Haus' (neuter) = house.
grammarNote: 'In der Nähe von' takes the dative case: 'von meinem Haus'. 'Die Apotheke' = the pharmacy; 'das Haus' = the house.

answer: How do I get to the museum?
alternatives: How can I get to the museum?
hint: 'Wie komme ich zu...?' is the standard way to ask for directions.
grammarNote: 'Zu + Dativ' for movement toward a destination. 'Zu dem Museum' contracts to 'zum Museum'. 'Das Museum' = the museum.

hint: Weather sentences in German always need a subject — 'es' (it).
grammarNote: German always uses 'es' as the subject for weather, even when English drops it. 'Es ist kalt/warm/heiß/kühl'.

prompt: It is raining and I have no umbrella.
answer: Es regnet und ich habe keinen Regenschirm.
hint: 'Regnen' = to rain. 'Der Regenschirm' is masculine, so accusative 'keinen'.
grammarNote: 'Es regnet' (it's raining) — no 'is' needed in German. 'Der Regenschirm' = the umbrella (masculine, so negation 'keinen' in accusative).

hint: 'In dem Winter' contracts to 'im Winter'. When you start with a time expression, the verb comes second.
grammarNote: 'Im Winter' (in winter) — preposition + dative. German is verb-second (V2): if 'Im Winter' takes position 1, the verb 'schneit' must be in position 2. 'Der Winter' = the winter.

prompt: Die Sonne scheint und es ist warm.
answer: The sun is shining and it is warm.
alternatives: The sun shines and it's warm.
hint: 'Scheinen' = to shine. 'Die Sonne' is feminine.
grammarNote: 'Die Sonne' = the sun. Note that German doesn't use a continuous tense ('-ing') — Präsens covers both 'shines' and 'is shining'.

prompt: I love summer because the days are long.
answer: Ich liebe den Sommer, weil die Tage lang sind.
alternatives: Ich mag den Sommer, weil die Tage lang sind.
hint: 'Weil' sends the verb to the end. 'Der Sommer' is masculine; in accusative it becomes 'den Sommer'.
grammarNote: 'Weil' is a subordinating conjunction — the finite verb goes to the very end: '...die Tage lang sind'. 'Der Sommer' = summer; the four seasons are all masculine: der Frühling, der Sommer, der Herbst, der Winter.

hint: 'Gern' after the verb expresses liking to do something. 'Das Buch' is neuter, plural 'Bücher'.
grammarNote: 'Verb + gern' = to like doing something. Position 'gern' right after the verb. 'Das Buch' (singular) → 'die Bücher' (plural).

prompt: My brother plays football every weekend.
answer: Mein Bruder spielt jedes Wochenende Fußball.
alternatives: Jedes Wochenende spielt mein Bruder Fußball.
hint: Sports with 'spielen' don't take an article: 'Fußball spielen', 'Tennis spielen'. 'Das Wochenende' is neuter.
grammarNote: Use 'spielen' for sports played with a ball, and skip the article: 'Fußball spielen', 'Tennis spielen', 'Basketball spielen'. 'Das Wochenende' = the weekend; 'jedes Wochenende' = every weekend (accusative time expression).

prompt: We swim every Saturday in the lake.
answer: Wir schwimmen jeden Samstag im See.
alternatives: Jeden Samstag schwimmen wir im See.
hint: 'Schwimmen' = to swim. 'Der See' is masculine; 'in dem See' contracts to 'im See'.
grammarNote: Time expressions in accusative: 'jeden Samstag'. Location with 'in + Dativ': 'im See'. 'Der See' = the lake (masculine) — not to be confused with 'die See' (the sea, feminine, less common).

prompt: Sie hört gern Musik und tanzt.
answer: She likes to listen to music and dances.
alternatives: She enjoys listening to music and dancing.
hint: 'Gern' attaches to the first verb but applies to both activities here.
grammarNote: 'Musik hören' = to listen to music (no preposition needed). 'Die Musik' = the music — but no article is used when speaking generally.

hint: For musical instruments with 'spielen', the article is optional in spoken German. 'Die Gitarre' is feminine.
grammarNote: 'Gitarre spielen' is the more common spoken form; 'die Gitarre spielen' is also correct. 'Die Gitarre' = the guitar. Other instruments: 'das Klavier' (piano), 'die Geige' (violin).

prompt: I think the film is good.
alternatives: Ich denke, der Film ist gut.
hint: 'Ich finde X gut' is the most common A1 way to express opinions. 'Der Film' becomes accusative 'den Film' after 'finden'.
grammarNote: 'Finden' (to find/think) takes accusative: 'Ich finde den Film gut'. 'Der Film' = the film (masculine) → accusative 'den Film'.

hint: 'Gefallen' works in reverse: the thing that pleases you is the subject, you are the dative object ('mir'). 'Das Lied' is neuter.
grammarNote: 'X gefällt mir' = I like X (literally: X is pleasing to me). The thing being liked is the grammatical subject. 'Das Lied' = the song.

prompt: I don't like the coffee — it tastes too bitter.
answer: Der Kaffee schmeckt mir nicht — er ist zu bitter.
alternatives: Mir schmeckt der Kaffee nicht, er ist zu bitter.
hint: 'Schmecken' (to taste) works like 'gefallen' — uses dative for the person.
grammarNote: 'Schmecken' is used for food/drink and takes a dative object: 'Mir schmeckt der Kaffee'. 'Der Kaffee' = the coffee; replaced by 'er' (masculine) in the second clause.

prompt: Ich finde diese Stadt sehr schön.
answer: I think this city is very beautiful.
alternatives: I find this city very beautiful.
hint: 'Finden' here means 'to think (about)' rather than 'to find'.
grammarNote: 'Finden' + accusative + adjective = think X is Y. 'Die Stadt' = the city (feminine) → accusative 'diese Stadt'.

prompt: The book is boring, but the film is interesting.
answer: Das Buch ist langweilig, aber der Film ist interessant.
hint: 'Aber' (but) is a coordinating conjunction — no word-order change.
grammarNote: After 'aber', normal word order continues (verb-second). 'Das Buch' = the book (neuter); 'der Film' = the film (masculine).

prompt: On Monday I have a meeting.
answer: Am Montag habe ich ein Meeting.
alternatives: Am Montag habe ich eine Besprechung.
hint: 'An dem Montag' contracts to 'am Montag'. When starting with a time, the verb comes second.
grammarNote: Days of the week take 'am' (an + dem) — they are all masculine: 'der Montag', 'der Dienstag', etc. V2 rule: 'Am Montag habe ich...' not 'Am Montag ich habe...'.

hint: 'In dem Mai' contracts to 'im Mai'. 'Der Geburtstag' is masculine.
grammarNote: Months take 'im' (in + dem). All months are masculine: 'der Januar', 'der Februar', etc. 'Der Geburtstag' = the birthday.

prompt: Today is the third of October.
hint: Ordinal dates take '-te' for 1-19 and '-ste' for 20+. 'Der dritte' is irregular.
grammarNote: Ordinal numbers in dates: 'der erste, der zweite, der dritte, der vierte...' — '1.', '2.', '3.', etc. with a period in writing. Note 'erste' and 'dritte' are irregular.

prompt: Am Sonntag besucht meine Familie meine Großeltern.
answer: On Sunday my family visits my grandparents.
alternatives: On Sunday, my family is visiting my grandparents.
hint: 'Am Sonntag' = on Sunday. Verb-second pushes 'meine Familie' after the verb.
grammarNote: When a time expression begins the sentence, the subject moves after the verb: 'Am Sonntag besucht meine Familie...'. 'Die Großeltern' (plural) = the grandparents.

prompt: We have a German class every Wednesday.
alternatives: Wir haben jeden Mittwoch eine Deutschstunde.
hint: 'Jeden Mittwoch' (every Wednesday) — accusative for time. 'Der Mittwoch' is masculine.
grammarNote: Time expressions denoting repeated occurrences use accusative: 'jeden Tag', 'jeden Montag'. 'Der Deutschunterricht' = the German lesson/class (compound noun, masculine like 'der Unterricht').
